 An apparatus using a model trained using artificial intelligence, comprising: The apparatus comprises one or more processors; The one or more processors: acquiring or receiving angiographic image data; receiving or loading a trained model from memory, the trained model having been trained using artificial intelligence with training data including at least angiographic frames of angiographic image data to at least detect marker locations on the angiographic frames of the acquired or received angiographic image data; applying the trained model to the acquired or received angiographic image data; selecting an angiographic frame of the one or more angiographic frames of the acquired or received angiographic image data after the trained model has been applied; detecting marker locations on the selected angiography frames using the trained model; (i) verifying whether the marker location is correct using output data from the trained model if the training data of the trained model further includes actual marker location data, and/or (ii) using input from a user of the device, wherein the output data of the trained model or the input from the user indicates whether the marker location is correct or indicates a correction to be made to the marker location if the marker location is not correct; if the marker location was not correct , modifying the detected marker location using the output data from the training model indicating the modification to be made to the marker location and/or using the input from the user of the device indicating the modification to be made to the marker location and repeating the checking whether the modified marker location is correct or, if the marker location is correct , checking whether all angiography frames of the one or more angiography frames have been checked for containing a correct marker location ; if all of the angiography frames have not been checked for containing a correct marker location , selecting another angiography frame of the one or more angiography frames and repeating the marker location detection and checking for the correct marker location for the other angiography frame; An apparatus that functions to perform the above .

Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is a technique for obtaining high-resolution cross-sectional images of tissues and materials, allowing real-time visualization. The goal of OCT techniques is to measure the time delay of light using interference optics and techniques such as Fourier transform and Michelson interferometers. Light from a light source is split by a splitter (e.g., a beam splitter) and sent to a reference arm and a sample (or measurement) arm. The reference beam is reflected from a reference mirror (partially reflective or other reflective element) in the reference arm, and the sample beam is reflected or scattered from the sample in the sample arm. Both beams are combined (or recombined) at the splitter, producing interference fringes. The output of the interferometer is detected by one or more detectors, such as photodiodes or multi-array cameras, in one or more devices, such as a spectrometer (e.g., a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer). Interference fringes are produced when the path length of the sample arm matches the path length of the reference arm within the coherence length of the light source. By evaluating the output beams, the spectrum of the input radiation can be derived as a function of frequency. The frequency of the interference fringes corresponds to the distance between the sample and reference arms. The higher the frequency, the greater the difference in path length. Single mode fiber can be used for OCT optical probes, and double clad fiber can be used for fluorescence and/or spectroscopy.

Percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) has been dramatically improved by innovative imaging modalities such as coronary angiography and intravascular imaging. Coronary angiography provides longitudinal silhouettes of the coronary arteries, while intravascular imaging modalities provide cross-sectional information of the coronary arteries. As intravascular imaging modalities such as intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) and optical coherence tomography (OCT) provide more precise information about vascular lesions (e.g., lumen size, plaque morphology, implanted devices), systems have been developed that allow physicians to link (i.e., coregist) ex vivo and in vivo imaging modalities. One currently available method requires the generation of vascular centerlines for coregistration from angiographic data acquired simultaneously during IVUS/OCT pullback. Another method requires the generation of imaging catheter pathways from angiographic data acquired prior to IVUS/OCT pullback via user input.

One or more embodiments of the present disclosure may apply machine learning (particularly deep learning) to identify one or more markers in angiographic image frames (e.g., one or more frames from a video, one or more frames from an image, etc.) without user input defining an area where intravascular imaging pullback occurs. For example, using artificial intelligence (e.g., (but not limited to) deep learning/machine learning, residual learning, computer vision tasks (keypoint or object detection, and/or image segmentation)), using a unique architectural structure of the model, using a unique training process, using input data creation techniques, using input mapping to the model, and using post-processing and interpretation of output data, one or more embodiments of the present disclosure may achieve a better or maximum success rate of marker detection from angiographic data without (or with less) user interaction, and may reduce processing and/or prediction time for displaying coregistration results based on marker detection results. In the present disclosure, a model may be defined as software that receives an image as input and returns a prediction of a specified image as output. In one or more embodiments, a model may be a specific instance of a model architecture (a set of parameter values) obtained by training and selecting a model using machine learning and/or optimization algorithms/processes. A model generally consists of or consists of the following parts: an architecture defined by source code (e.g., a convolutional neural network composed of layers of parameterized convolution kernels and activation functions) and configuration values (parameters, weights or features) that are initially set to random values and then iteratively optimized during training given data examples (e.g., image-label pairs), an objective function (loss function), and an optimization algorithm (optimizer).

One or more embodiments of the present disclosure can achieve efficient marker detection and/or efficient coregistration results by creating an identifier or detector to detect radiopaque markers of an intravascular imaging catheter from an angiographic image. In one or more embodiments, angiographic data can be acquired during an intravascular imaging pullback with a catheter having radiopaque markers that can be visualized in the angiographic image. In one or more embodiments, the ground truth identifies the location of the radiopaque markers. In one or more embodiments, the model (which in one or more embodiments can be software, a combination of software/hardware, or a procedure that utilizes one or more machine learning or deep learning algorithms/procedures/processes trained on data to make one or more predictions about future unseen data) has sufficient resolution to predict marker locations with sufficient accuracy depending on the application or procedure being performed. The performance of the model can be further improved by later adding more training data and retraining the model to create a new instance of the model with improved or optimized performance. For example, the additional training data can include data based on user input, where a user can identify or correct the location of the radiopaque markers in the image. One or more embodiments may use an identifier or detector to detect the radiopaque markers.

In one or more embodiments, the one or more processors may further function to split the ground truth data into training, validation and testing sets or groups. The one or more processors may be further operable to perform one or more of the following: (i) detecting or identifying markers or radiopaque markers in the angiographic image data based on the generated identifiers or detectors; (ii) calculating or improving a marker detection success rate using application of machine learning or deep learning; (iii) determining a model to train based on the marker detection success rate associated with the model (e.g., in device or system embodiments, if there are multiple stored models (already trained previously), the device/system method may select a model for further training based on, for example, previous success rates, based on predefined success factors, or based on which model is more optimal than another model); (iv) calculating a co-registration success rate and/or determining whether the location of the detected marker is accurate based on the trained model; and (v) evaluating the marker detection success rate and/or the co-registration success rate using the root mean square error between the predicted and actual locations of the markers. In one or more embodiments, the one or more processors may be further operable to perform one or more of the following: (i) splitting the acquired or received angiographic image data into data sets or groups in a particular ratio or proportion, e.g., 70% training data, 15% validation data, and 15% test data; (ii) randomly splitting the acquired or received angiographic image data; (iii) randomly splitting the acquired or received angiographic image data based on either a pullback criterion or a frame criterion; (iv) dividing the acquired or received angiographic image data based on or with a new set of specific or predetermined types of data. (v) segmenting the acquired or received angiographic image data based on or using a new set of specific or predetermined data types, the new set being one or more of a new pullback reference data set, a new frame reference data set, new clinical data, new animal data, new potential additional training data, new data of a first type of catheter having markers similar to markers of a catheter used in the acquired or received angiographic image data, and new data having markers similar to markers of an optical coherence tomography (OCT) catheter. The one or more processors may be further operable to perform one or more of the following: (i) employing data quality control; (ii) allowing a user to manually select training samples or training data; (iii) allowing a user to identify markers or targets to be detected and use such samples as data points for model training; (iv) using any angio images captured during the optical coherence tomography (OCT) pullback for testing. In one or more embodiments, the one or more processors may be further operable to perform one or more of the following: (i) performing image preprocessing; (ii) performing image preprocessing by normalizing pixel values; and (iii) performing image preprocessing by normalizing pixel values for each individual angio frame before training begins and/or for each batch of angio frames input to the model at each iteration of training. The one or more processors may be operable to perform one or more of the following after an architecture or configuration of the model for training has been selected by a user or the system based on predetermined criteria and the type of data, inputs and outputs to be used have been determined: (i) when the model is a segmentation or classification model, the inputs are individual angio image frames and the output is the corresponding segmented, labelled or masked images; (ii) when the model is a segmentation or classification model, the inputs are individual angio image frames and the output is the corresponding segmented, labelled or masked images such that foreground pixels defining marker regions have positive values and background pixels have zero values. (iii) when the model is an object detection model or a regression model, the input is an individual angio image frame and the output is a corresponding set of spatial coordinates defining the marker location or target marker; and (iv) when the model performs a combination of segmentation (pixel classification) and/or object detection (spatial coordinate point regression), the input includes a combination of individual angio frames and the output includes a combination of one or more of the following: a segmented or masked image; a segmented or masked image in which foreground pixels defining the marker region have positive values and background pixels have zero values; and marker location coordinates or target marker coordinates. In one or more embodiments, the segmentation model can use post-processing after obtaining the segmented or masked image to determine the marker location coordinate points.

One or more embodiments of a method for detecting and/or performing co-registration of markers or radiopaque markers in angiographic image data may include: acquiring or receiving angiographic image data; receiving a trained model or loading the trained model from memory; applying the trained model to the acquired or received angiographic image data; selecting an angiographic frame; detecting a marker position on the selected angiographic frame using the trained model, the marker position defining a detection result; verifying whether the marker position is accurate or correct; if the marker position is not accurate or correct, modifying the detection result or the detected marker position and repeating the verification of whether the marker position is accurate or correct, or, if the marker position is accurate or correct, verifying whether all of the angiographic frames have been verified for accuracy or correctness; and, if all of the angiographic frames have not been verified for accuracy or correctness, selecting another angiographic frame and repeating the detection of the marker position and the verification of whether the marker position is accurate or correct for the another angiographic frame. The method may include one or more of the following: (i) performing coregistration based on the detected marker positions when all of the angiographic frames have been checked for accuracy or correctness; (ii) displaying the detected marker positions on a display; (iii) displaying the detected marker positions on a display such that the detected marker positions are superimposed on the angiographic data; (iv) displaying the modified detection results and/or the modified marker positions on a display; (v) inserting an intravascular imaging catheter having a marker or a radiopaque marker into the object or sample; and (vi) acquiring or receiving angiographic image data during a pullback movement of the intravascular imaging catheter.
